ocr.text: The deployment of satellite constellations represents a critical component of advancing European capabilities in aerospace and
defense. As outlined in the recent analysis, the total number of satellites planned for operational deployment is indicative of our
strategic objectives. Notably, the LaunchSchedule’s TotalSatellites is projected to include multiple phases, with the first set
anticipated for completion by Q1, followed by additional launches in Q3, ensuring a phased approach to operational readiness.
The Operational column reveals that, by the end of this schedule, 8 satellites will be fully operational across various missions,
contributing to enhanced data collection and communication services.
Year  DeploymentStatus
Metric  2020  2021
TotalSatellites  12  15  20
Operational  [HIDDEN]  10  [HIDDEN]
UnderConstruction  4  5  6
Planned  0  0  0
[HIDDEN]  [HIDDEN]  [HIDDEN]  Q2
Budget(CHF)  150M  200M  250M
LaunchSuccessRate  75%  80%  85%
KeyPartnerships  ESA  NASA  ISRO
DataThroughput  50Gbps  [HIDDEN]  70Gbps
TechnologyUpgrades  Yes  Yes  Yes
Additionally, the DataThroughput metric highlights the substantial capacity these satellites will provide, boasting an impressive
potential of 60Gbps, which is essential for meeting the growing demands of data transmission in both civilian and defense
sectors. The comprehensive analysis also indicates that, over time, a total of 20 satellites will be integrated into the
constellation, with an operational count reaching 14 by the completion of the deployment. This strategic initiative not only aims
to bolster national security but also seeks to enhance collaborative efforts across European nations in satellite-based intelligence
and reconnaissance. The financial implications of this deployment are significant, with projected costs in the realm of CHF
billions, reinforcing the necessity for meticulous planning and execution to achieve optimal results in the realm of aerospace
advancements.
